issues.claim: The dhīra — steady, not abstractly 'wise' — is the operator whose nervous system does not flare at rewrite-time
issues.objection: Dhīra in the Sanskrit means 'firm, steady, intelligent, sagacious.' The engineering rendering 'steady, not abstractly wise' correctly preserves the steadiness aspect that Prabhupada's 'sober person' picks up and that Mukundananda's 'wise' loses. This is good translation discipline — choosing the technical narrowness over the generic. No distortion; if anything, this preserves a doctrinal nuance that modern English translations flatten.

notes: The engineering layer accurately preserves the verse's analogical structure: just as the embodied passes through state transitions (childhood → youth → old age) without identity-collapse, so it passes to another body without identity-collapse. The engineering analog (v1 → v2 → v3 → v4 of the same service) is structurally faithful to the Sanskrit's analogical argument. Critically, the engineering layer does NOT collapse this into 'reincarnation' (which would be a sectarian commitment) and does NOT extend it beyond what the Sanskrit warrants. The dhīra-tatra-na-muhyati — 'the steady-minded is not bewildered regarding that' — is preserved as the precise diagnostic of the wise: not detached, not numb, but located at the right level for stability. The conformance test suite as 'the surviving artifact across transitions' is a register-faithful analog of what holds across the dehāntara-prāptiḥ. PASS at high confidence.
